records.ety: [F. failir, fr. L. fallere, falsum, to deceive, akin to E. fall. See Fail, and cf. Fallacy, False, Fault.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Failed (fāld); p. pr. & vb. n. Failing.]

defs: To be wanting; to fall short; to be or become deficient in any measure or degree up to total absence; to cease to be furnished in the usual or expected manner, or to be altogether cut off from supply; to be lacking; as, streams fail; crops fail.
quotes: As the waters fail from the sea.
Till Lionel's issue fails, his should not reign.

defs: To be affected with want; to come short; to lack; to be deficient or unprovided; -- used with of.
quotes: If ever they fail of beauty, this failure is not be attributed to their size.

defs: To fall away; to become diminished; to decline; to decay; to sink.
quotes: When earnestly they seek Such proof, conclude they then begin to fail.

defs: To deteriorate in respect to vigor, activity, resources, etc.; to become weaker; as, a sick man fails.
To perish; to die; -- used of a person.
quotes: Had the king in his last sickness failed.

defs: To be found wanting with respect to an action or a duty to be performed, a result to be secured, etc.; to miss; not to fulfill expectation.
quotes: Take heed now that ye fail not to do this.
Either my eyesight fails, or thou look'st pale.

defs: To come short of a result or object aimed at or desired ; to be baffled or frusrated.
To err in judgment; to be mistaken.
quotes: Which ofttimes may succeed, so as perhaps Shall grieve him, if I fail not.

defs: To become unable to meet one's engagements; especially, to be unable to pay one's debts or discharge one's business obligation; to become bankrupt or insolvent; as, many credit unions failed in the late 1980's.
To be wanting to ; to be insufficient for; to disappoint; to desert.
quotes: There shall not fail thee a man on the throne.

defs: To miss of attaining; to lose.
quotes: Though that seat of earthly bliss be failed.

records.ety: [OF. faille, from failir. See Fail, v. i.]

defs: Miscarriage; failure; deficiency; fault; -- mostly superseded by failure or failing, except in the phrase without fail.
